mainTopic: (continues) תני רבי חייא מנה לי בידך
overview: The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Chiya's teaching on partial admission and witnesses, exploring the kal vachomer logic of Rav Papa regarding why witnesses obligate an oath just as a partial admission does. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.topic: (continues) תני רבי חייא מנה לי בידך
sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Chiya's teaching on partial admission and witnesses, exploring the kal vachomer logic of Rav Papa regarding why witnesses obligate an oath just as a partial admission does. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Explaining the קַל וָחוֹמֶר
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what the kal vachomer actually is, setting up the explanation of the logical inference.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא asks a clarifying question about the structure of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. This is a setup line for the detailed analysis.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks what the קַל וָחוֹמֶר is. It then formulates the inference: If the mouth, which doesn't obligate monetary payment (in some sense), obligates an oath, then witnesses, which do obligate monetary payment, should certainly obligate an oath.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the detailed analysis of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר's premises.

phrases.english: And what is the a fortiori inference?
And what — his mouth that does not obligate him money —

phrases.aramaic: מְחַיְּיבוֹ שְׁבוּעָה, עֵדִים שֶׁמְּחַיְּיבִין אוֹתוֹ מָמוֹן –
phrases.english: obligates him an oath, witnesses that obligate him money —
is it not logical that they obligate him an oath?

rashi.hebrew: פיו שאינו מחייבו ממון - בהודאתו ולקמיה מפרש לה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase 'פיו שאינו מחייבו ממון' refers to the defendant's admission, which does not obligate monetary payment, and notes that this will be explained further in the Gemara.
רש״י clarifies that 'מחייבו שבועה' means the defendant's admission obligates an oath regarding the remainder of the claim.
רש״י explains that 'עדים שמחייבין אותו ממון' refers to witnesses who obligate monetary payment for the amount they testified about.

rashi.hebrew: אינו דין שיחייבוהו שבועה - על השאר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'אינו דין שיחייבוהו שבועה' means it is logical that witnesses should also obligate an oath regarding the remainder of the claim.

steps.title: Challenge to the premise that admission doesn't obligate money
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the premise of the kal vachomer that the mouth doesn't obligate monetary payment, citing the principle that a defendant's admission is as strong as a hundred witnesses.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the formulation of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It questions the claim that the mouth doesn't obligate monetary payment.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks: But doesn't the mouth obligate monetary payment? It cites the principle הוֹדָאַת בַּעַל דִּין כְּמֵאָה עֵדִים דָּמֵי — the admission of a litigant is equivalent to a hundred witnesses. If admission is as strong as witnesses, and witnesses obligate payment, then admission should also obligate payment. This challenges the premise of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר that the mouth is less stringent regarding monetary liability.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to refine the definition of 'monetary liability' in the context of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר.

phrases.english: And his mouth does not obligate him money?

phrases.aramaic: וְהָא הוֹדָאַת בַּעַל דִּין כְּמֵאָה עֵדִים דָּמֵי!
phrases.english: And isn't the admission of a litigant like one hundred witnesses?

rashi.hebrew: הודאת בעל דין כמאה עדים - ברייתא היא בתוספתא דבבא מציעא בפרק ראשון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle הוֹדָאַת בַּעַל דִּין כְּמֵאָה עֵדִים דָּמֵי is derived from a ברייתא in the תוספתא of בבא מציעא.

steps.title: Clarifying that 'money' refers to a fine
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by specifying that 'money' in the kal vachomer refers to a fine (kenas), which admission doesn't obligate but witnesses do.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the challenge by refining the definition of 'monetary liability' in the קַל וָחוֹמֶר.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא explains that 'money' in the קַל וָחוֹמֶר refers to a קְנָס. In cases of fines, a defendant's admission doesn't obligate payment, but witnesses do. Therefore, the קַל וָחוֹמֶר is: If the mouth, which doesn't obligate a fine, obligates an oath, then witnesses, which do obligate a fine, should certainly obligate an oath. This resolves the challenge that admission is as strong as witnesses, because in the specific context of fines, admission is weaker.
steps.whyThisMatters: This clarifies the precise legal distinction being made in the קַל וָחוֹמֶר.

phrases.english: What is 'money' — a fine.

phrases.aramaic: וּמָה פִּיו שֶׁאֵין מְחַיְּיבוֹ קְנָס, מְחַיְּיבוֹ שְׁבוּעָה!
phrases.english: And what? His mouth, which does not obligate him a fine, obligates him an oath!
Witnesses that obligate him a fine,
Is it not a fortiori that they obligate him an oath!

rashi.hebrew: פיו אינו מחייבו קנס - דילפינן (ב"ק דף עה.) מקראי דמודה בקנס פטור אשר ירשיעון אלהים פרט למרשיע את עצמו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle that a defendant's admission does not obligate a fine is derived from the verse in בבא קמא, which excludes one who condemns himself from the category of those whom God condemns.

steps.title: Challenge from the obligation to bring a sacrifice
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the kal vachomer by noting that admission obligates bringing a sacrifice, while witnesses don't, which might weaken the inference.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new difficulty against the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It points out a case where admission is more stringent than witnesses.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks: What is notable about the mouth? It obligates bringing a קָרְבָּן. If a person admits to a transgression, he must bring a sacrifice. But witnesses don't obligate a sacrifice if the person denies the transgression. This challenges the קַל וָחוֹמֶר's premise that witnesses are more stringent than admission. If admission is more stringent in some cases (sacrifices), why should witnesses obligate an oath?

keyTerms.meaning: What is notable about the mouth

steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to address the relationship between admission and witnesses in the context of sacrifices.

phrases.english: What is notable about the admission of one's mouth?
A neighbor obligates him to bring an offering,

phrases.aramaic: תֹּאמַר בְּעֵדִים שֶׁאֵין מְחַיְּיבִין אוֹתוֹ קׇרְבָּן!
phrases.english: Would you say about witnesses that they do not obligate him to bring an offering!

rashi.hebrew: פיו מחייבו קרבן - דכתיב והתודה אשר חטא והביא (ויקרא ה׳:ה׳) עדים אין מחייבין אותו קרבן אם מכחישין אותו דכתיב (שם ד) או הודע אליו ולא שיודיעוהו אחרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that פיו מחייבו קרבן is derived from the verse 'והתודה אשר חטא והביא', meaning a person's own admission obligates a sacrifice, whereas witnesses do not obligate a sacrifice if the person denies them, as the verse requires 'או הודע אליו' (it was made known to him) and not that others inform him.

tosafot.hebrew: מה לפיו שכן מחייבו קרבן תאמר בעדים כו' - פי' שכן מחייבו קרבן אפילו העדים מכחישין אותו ואומרים לאו חלב הוה אלא שומן דאי אין העדים מכחישין אותו היכי קאמר תאמר בעדים כו' עדים נמי מחייבין אותו קרבן אם אינו מכחישן ואפילו עד אחד אם אינו מכחישו מחייבו קרבן וזאת הסוגיא סוברת כלישנא דפרק אמרו לו (כריתות דף יב. ושם) דמפרש טעמייהו דרבנן דלקמן דאמרו לו אכלת חלב והוא אומר לא אכלתי דפטור משום דאדם נאמן על עצמו יותר ממאה איש ולכך פיו מחייבו קרבן אפילו עדים מכחישין אותו ועדים אין מחייבין אותו קרבן אם מכחישן דלאידך לישנא דמפרש טעמייהו דרבנן דמתרץ דיבוריה ואמר דלא אכלתי שוגג אלא מזיד ומיירי דכשאמר לא אכלתי לא בדקנו אותו היאך היה דעתו אבל להכחיש העדים אינו נאמן א"כ חשיבי עדים מפיו שכן חייבוהו קרבן אפי' מכחישן והוא לא מחייב עצמו קרבן אם היו מכחישין אותו ואתי רבי חייא אפילו כרבנן:
tosafot.english: What [can you prove from the admission] of his [own] mouth, which [has the strength to] obligate him [to bring] a karban? Can the same be said for witnesses etc. [who do not have the power to obligate him to bring a karban]? At this point in the dialogue of the Gemara, the Gemara is using one’s mouth - his admission - as the basis of a kal v’chomer. His admission does not have the strength to obligate him to pay penalties, and even so it obligates him to swear when he admits that he owes fifty of the one hundred zuz the plaintiff is claiming. Certainly, the testimony of witnesses which is strong enough to obligate him to pay penalties, should also have the strength to require him to swear when that testimony says that he owes fifty of the hundred zuz that the plaintiff is claiming. Penalties His admission: No Witnesses: Yes Oath His admission: Yes Witnesses: Certainly As we explained in the previous Tosfos, a kal v’chomer is subject to a ‘פירכא - challenge’. The nature of the challenge is to show that one’s mouth, which is being described as the ‘קל - lenient’ is actually more powerful in certain cases. Presently, the Gemara argues that one’s mouth is more powerful than the testimony of witnesses, because his admission can obligate him to bring a karban. Presumably, the logical conclusion of this sentence is, but witnesses cannot obligate him to offer a karban. Tosfos will explain the exact conditions where one’s admission is more powerful than the testimony of witnesses in regard to offering a karban. When the Gemara says that one’s admission obligates him to offer a karban, the meaning is: That his admission obligates him to offer a karban even when witnesses contradict him and say: What you ate was not cheilev,1Cheilev refers to the fats of the animal that are prohibited by the pain of kares. but shuman.2Shuman refers to other fatty parts of the animal that are permitted for consumption. In this case, his admission wins and he is obligated to offer a karban, even though witnesses testify that he is not required to offer a karban. Perhaps the Gemara is speaking of one’s admission that he is liable to offer a karban when it is not contradicted by witnesses? For if the witnesses are not contradicting him, how can [the Gemara] say: Can you say the same for witnesses etc. who do not obligate him to bring a karban?]. But if we are discussing one’s admission when it is not contradicted by witnesses,it is simply not true that witness’ testimony would not obligate a karban in a comparable situation. The reality is that witnesses also obligate him to bring a karban, if he does not contradict them. For even a single witness, if not contradicted, obligates him to bring a karban. When not contradicted, an admission, testimony of witnesses and even a single witness are all equal as far as obligating him to offer a karban. The special strength of an admission must be that even if it is contradicted by witnesses, it still obligates him to offer a karban. In order to understand this Tosfos properly we must learn a Gemara in Kereisos 12a that discusses the reason why one’s own admission stands even against the testimony of witnesses. The Gemara there is discussing the Mishna in Kereisos 11b, soon to be quoted in our Gemara about a dispute between R’ Meir and the Rabanan. R’ Meir holds that if two witnesses testify that a person ate cheilev he must offer a karban. The Rabanan say the he is exempt. The Gemara there asks: What is the reason of the Rabanan? Is it because a person is believed about himself more than a hundred people, since this is something that affects only himself, he is the ultimate authority about his own obligations? Or perhaps it is because we say there is a migo, that he could have said “I ate the cheilev intentionally”. It is beyond the witnesses to know whether he ate the cheilev intentionally or inadvertently. Thus, if he would insist that he ate it intentionally, we would necessarily believe him. So too, when he says: I did not eat cheilev at all, he is believed and is exempt from bringing a karban. We see that there are two possible explanations of the Rabanan’s opinion: a) A person is believed about his own private affairs even to the exclusion of witnesses. b) We believe him because he could have exempted himself by saying: I ate the cheilev intentionally. Which of these two versions in Kereisos 12a is compatible with our Gemara in Bava Metzia 3b? This passage holds like the version in Perek Amru Lo (Kereisos 12a) that explains the reason of the Rabanan soon quoted later, that when [witnesses] said to him: You ate cheilev and he says I did not eat cheilev, that he is exempt from offering a karban, because a person is believed about himself more than a hundred people. Therefore, his mouth obligates him to offer a karban even if witnesses contradict him3See אוצר מפרשי התלמוד who quotes many commentators who explain Tosfos’ position in this issue. We must keep in mind that when a person claims that he ate cheilev and witnesses contradict him, he may be believed as far as himself is concerned. In reality however, there are other people affected by this question. Consider the Kohanim who need to offer this sacrifice. It is true that the supposed perpetrator insists that he is obligated to offer this karban, but there are a pair of kosher witnesses who say that there is no obligation to offer this karban and it is actually an ordinary animal that is not a karban at all. So too, as far as eating the flesh of this karban, the witnesses are saying that it is forbidden to do so, because this is חולין שנשחטו בעזרה - an ordinary animal that was slaughtered in the temple, which may not be eaten. It is beyond the scope of this work to deal with all these issues. because a person is the ultimate authority about himself. However, by the same token witnesses do not obligate him to offer a karban, if he contradicts their testimony. Why is our Gemara not compatible with approach b) mentioned in Kereisos 12a? For according to the other version, that explains the reason of the Rabanan that we believe the person who says: I did not eat cheilev by adjusting his statement and explaining his words as follows: I did not eat cheilev inadvertently which would make me liable for a karban, rather, I did so intentionally and I am exempt from a karban.4See רמב"ם שגגות פ'א ה'א who rules in favor of this opinion. This version b) of the explanation of the Rabanan must be logical. Tosfos adds something to the simple meaning of that Gemara: It must be speaking of when at the time he said “I did not eat cheilev” we did not interrogate him about what he meant. Since we do not have a clear statement that he did not eat cheilev at all, we can say that initially he meant that he did not eat cheilev inadvertently, but intentionally and he is therefore exempt from a karban. However, he is not believed to directly contradict the witnesses. If he should say that he did not eat cheilev at all and the witnesses testify that he did eat cheilev inadvertently, we would not believe him and he would have to bring a karban. If so, the testimony of witnesses is more powerful than his mouth, for they can obligate him to offer a karban even when he contradicts them, and he cannot obligate himself to offer a karban, if [witnesses] were contradicting him. According to this version in the Gemara in Kereisos, R’ Chiya’s position that the testimony of witnesses is stronger than one’s own admission comes out even according to the Rabanan. Our Gemara did not need to say that R’ Chiya follows R’ Meir who disagrees with the Rabanan.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that the Gemara's challenge assumes the opinion in כריתות that a person is more trustworthy than a hundred witnesses regarding their own actions, so פיו מחייבו קרבן even if witnesses contradict him, whereas witnesses cannot obligate a sacrifice if he contradicts them; this differs from the alternative opinion where witnesses would obligate a sacrifice if he doesn't contradict them, which would invalidate this specific kal vachomer challenge.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות builds on רש״י's basic distinction by specifying that this challenge only works according to the opinion in כריתות that a person's denial overrides witnesses regarding personal knowledge, ensuring that פיו is indeed more stringent than עדים in the context of קרבן.

steps.title: Resolving via רַבִּי מֵאִיר's opinion
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by attributing Rabbi Chiya's view to Rabbi Meir, who holds that witnesses do obligate a sacrifice via a kal vachomer.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the challenge by citing רַבִּי מֵאִיר's opinion that witnesses do obligate a sacrifice.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא explains that רַבִּי חִיָּה holds like רַבִּי מֵאִיר. רַבִּי מֵאִיר argues that if witnesses can obligate a severe penalty (death), they should certainly obligate a light penalty (sacrifice). The רַבָּנִים object that the defendant might claim he sinned intentionally, which exempts him from a sacrifice. But רַבִּי מֵאִיר maintains that witnesses obligate a sacrifice. Therefore, according to רַבִּי חִיָּה/רַבִּי מֵאִיר, witnesses are indeed more stringent than admission in the context of sacrifices, and the קַל וָחוֹמֶר holds.
steps.whyThisMatters: This resolves the challenge by showing that witnesses are indeed more stringent than admission in the context of sacrifices, according to רַבִּי מֵאִיר.

phrases.english: This is not difficult:

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי חִיָּיא כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר סְבִירָא לֵיהּ, דְּאָמַר:
phrases.english: Rabbi Ḥiyya holds in accordance with Rabbi Meir, who says:

phrases.aramaic: עֵדִים מְחַיְּיבִין אוֹתוֹ קׇרְבָּן מִקַּל וָחוֹמֶר.
phrases.english: Witnesses obligate him to bring an offering by a fortiori inference.
As we learned: Two said to him, "You ate fat,"
and he says, "I did not eat,"
Rabbi Meir obligates, and the Sages exempt.
Rabbi Meir said:

phrases.aramaic: אִם הֱבִיאוּהוּ שְׁנַיִם לִידֵי מִיתָה חֲמוּרָה,
phrases.english: If two brought him to the severe death penalty,

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יְבִיאוּהוּ לִידֵי קׇרְבָּן הַקַּל? אָמְרוּ לוֹ:
phrases.english: will they not bring him to the light offering? They said to him:

phrases.aramaic: מָה אִם יִרְצֶה לוֹמַר ״מֵזִיד הָיִיתִי״ – יִפָּטֵר.
phrases.english: What if he would want to say, "I was intentional" — he would be exempt.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָא לָא קַשְׁיָא: רַבִּי חִיָּיא כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר סְבִירָא לֵיהּ

rashi.hebrew: אמרו לו שנים אכלת חלב - שוגג:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the witnesses' accusation of eating חלב implies the defendant acted שוגג, which is the prerequisite for being obligated in a קרבן.

rashi.hebrew: והוא אומר לא [אכלתי] - פטור שהיה יכול לומר מזיד הייתי ויפטר מן הקרבן אף כשאמר לא אכלתי פטור דמה לו לשקר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the חכמים exempt the defendant because he could have claimed מזיד to avoid the קרבן, so his denial of eating at all is treated as a denial of שוגג, rendering him פטור.

tosafot.hebrew: רבי חייא כר"מ סבירא ליה - וא"ת א"כ נימא ק"ו דר"מ אם הביאוהו לידי מיתה לא יביאוהו לידי שבועה הקלה וי"ל דאיכא למפרך דמה למיתה שכן על מה שהן מעידין הוא מת תאמר גבי שבועה שעל מה שהוא כופר הוא נשבע כדאמרי' לקמן:
tosafot.english: R’ Chiya holds like R’ Meir [who says that witnesses can also obligate one to bring a karban]. Let us remind ourselves that we are discussing A) R’ Chiya’s kal v’chomer that the testimony of witnesses is stronger than one’s own admission because their testimony can obligate him to pay fines, which his own admission cannot accomplish. If his own admission can obligate him to swear upon the fifty zuz that he is denying, witnesses’ testimony that he owes fifty zuz, which is stronger than his own admission should also obligate him to swear on the other fifty zuz. Penalties Kal, His admission: No Chomer, Witnesses: Yes Oath Kal, His admission: Yes Chomer, Witnesses: Certainly The Gemara challenges this kal v’chomer by saying that in a way one’s mouth is stronger than witnesses because it can obligate him to bring a karban, which witnesses cannot do. Thus the perceived kal his admission - is not actually kal, because it has the power to accomplish what the chomer - witnesses cannot do. The Gemara responds that R’ Chiya follows the opinion of R’ Meir who says that witnesses can in fact obligate one to bring a karban. Thus his admission remains - kal - in comparison to witnesses’ testimony. R’ Meir’s ruling is also kal v’chomer based: B) If the testimony of witnesses is strong enough to execute a person, it should certainly be strong enough to obligate him to bring a karban, which is a much lighter punishment than death. Note, that this is a shortened version of the type of kal v’chomer A) mentioned earlier in the Gemara. We are not drawing a comparison from one’s admission to witnesses’ testimony. We are simply saying that since witnesses’ testimony can establish liability for capital punishment, it should certainly be capable of establishing liability for a karban. R’ Meir’s kal v’chomer: Witnesses can effect capital punishment. Therefore, witnesses should certainly effect liability for a karban. What is emerging is that R’ Chiya is teaching us kal v’chomer A) which is based on what is derived from kal v’chomer B). Since kal v’chomer B) teaches us that witnesses can obligate one to bring a karban, one’s own admission remains kal by comparison and we can now learn from A) the first longer kal v’chomer that witnesses’ testimony, which is the relative chomer, should obligate an oath. Tosfos wonders why we cannot directly apply the underlying basis of kal v’chomer B) to the issue of witnesses obligating a defendant to swear. The chomer of kal v’chomer B) is that witness’ testimony has the strength to effect capital punishment. We could also say: If the testimony of witnesses can establish liability for capital punishment, it should certainly establish an obligation to swear. Tosfos’ propsed kal v’chomer: Witnesses can effect capital punishment. Therefore, witnesses should also effect an obligation to swear. If you ask: If so, that R’ Chiya follows R’ Meir, who holds that witnesses can obligate one to bring a karban, which is based on kal v’chomer B), let us say that very same kal v’chomer of R’ Meir to learn that the testimony of witnesses can obligate an oath like so: If by their testimony [witnesses] can establish liability for capital punishment, they should certainly establish liability for an oath which is relatively lenient as compared to capital punishment? Why does R’ Chiya in his kal v’chomer draw from the comparison of one’s admission, which cannot cause him to pay fines to the testimony of witnesses, which can obligate him to pay fines? It seems that the direct kal v’chomer from witnesses establishing liability for capital punishment is more direct and to the point. We can answer: Because this kal v’chomer using the strength of witnesses’ testimony to establish liability for capital punishment as the basis for proving that their testimony should also obligate an oath, can be challenged as follows: For what can you prove from the witnesses’ strength to bring about capital punishment, where the defendant is punished for committing the crime that they are directly testifying about? Can the same be said in regard to obligating the defendant to swear, where he will swear about the fifty zuz of the claim that he denies which is not supported by the testimony of the witnesses, as the Gemara says later (at the end of this page)? It is only by comparing the testimony of witnesses to one’s own admission where we can draw a kal v’chomer which proves that the testimony of witnesses can establish an obligation to swear just as one’s own admission can establish such liability, despite the fact that he will be swearing about the unsupported portion of the claim.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות anticipates a challenge to רבי מאיר's קל וחומר: if witnesses can obligate מיתה, why not a שבועה, and answers that a שבועה is fundamentally different because it is sworn on a denial, unlike מיתה which is based on the witnesses' testimony.

tosafot.hebrew: מה אם ירצה לומר מזיד הייתי - ללישנא דאדם נאמן על עצמו יותר מק' עדים האי אם ירצה לומר לדבריו דר"מ קאמרי. (כדתניא) [כדאיתא] בפרק אמרו לו (כריתות דף יב. ושם) דלאידך לישנא טעמא דנפשייהו קאמרי ואין לפרש ומה אם ירצה לומר כו' שיהא נאמן לומר לא אכלתי מגו דאי בעי אמר מזיד הייתי דהא מגו במקום עדים הוא אלא כלומר אף מה שאמר לא אכלתי יכול לתרץ דבריו ולומר לא אכלתי שוגג אלא מזיד ומיירי דכשאמר לא אכלתי לא בדקו אותו איך היה דעתו ולבסוף אומר שמזיד היה וסברי רבנן דמה שאמר לא אכלתי כך היה דעתו שלא אכל שוגג אלא מזיד ולר"מ אע"פ שלבסוף אמר מזיד הייתי אין בכך כלום דהכי אמר (בתורת כהנים) אמר רבי מאיר אם בתחלה אמר מזיד הייתי שומעין לו אם היה דן עמו כל היום ובאחרונה אמר מזיד הייתי אין שומעין לו ואם תאמר והיאך נאמן לומר מזיד הייתי הא אין אדם משים עצמו רשע כדאמר בפ"ק דסנהדרין (דף ט: ושם) גבי פלוני רבעני לרצוני וי"ל דאין נאמן לפסול עצמו אבל הכא עושה תשובה ואינו רוצה להביא חולין לעזרה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that the חכמים' objection 'מה אם ירצה לומר מזיד הייתי' is not based on מגו, but rather on the principle that a person is believed regarding their own intent, meaning his initial denial is interpreted as a claim of מזיד, which רבי מאיר rejects if the claim of מזיד comes only after the initial denial.

meforshim.interplaySummary: While רש״י focuses on the mechanics of the חכמים' exemption based on the defendant's ability to claim מזיד, תוספות delves deeper into the logical structure of the objections, clarifying that the חכמים' objection is not a מגו but a matter of intent, and addressing why רבי מאיר's קל וחומר doesn't extend to שבועה.

steps.title: Challenge from אָשָׁם
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara raises a new difficulty: admission obligates a guilt-offering (asham), while witnesses don't, which might weaken the kal vachomer.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a final difficulty against the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It points out a case where הוֹדָאַת פִּיו is more stringent than הַעֲדָאַת עֵדִים.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks: What is notable about the mouth? It obligates an אָשָׁם. If a person admits to robbery, he must bring a guilt-offering. But witnesses don't obligate a guilt-offering if the person denies the robbery. This challenges the קַל וָחוֹמֶר's premise that witnesses are more stringent than admission.

keyTerms.meaning: What is notable about the mouth

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is from the case of אָשָׁם, where admission is more stringent than witnesses.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is challenging the קַל וָחוֹמֶר's structure, not its conclusion.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to address the relationship between admission and witnesses in the context of guilt-offerings.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא: מָה לְפִיו – שֶׁכֵּן מְחַיְּיבוֹ אָשָׁם!
phrases.english: Rather: What is notable about the mouth's admission? — A neighbor obligates him to a guilt-offering!
A guilt-offering is precisely an offering.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא: מָה לְפִיו – שֶׁכֵּן מְחַיְּבוֹ אָשָׁם!

tosafot.hebrew: אשם היינו קרבן - דלרבי מאיר עדים מחייבין אותו אשם תימה דבמתני' דד' שומרין (שבועות דף מט. ושם) תנן היכן שורי נגנב משביעך אני ואמר אמן והעדים מעידים אותו שאכלו משלם כפל הודה מעצמו משלם קרן וחומש ואשם אבל על פי עדים אין משלם חומש ואשם וכן תנן בפרק הגוזל קמא (ב"ק דף קח.) ומוקי לה בפרק הגוזל קמא כר"מ וי"ל דסוגיא דהכא דלרבי מאיר עדים מחייבין אותו אשם לא יעמיד ההיא משנה כר"מ אלא כדבעי לאוקמה התם כשאכלו טריפה וההיא סוגיא דהתם דסבר דלרבי מאיר עדים לא מחייבי אשם משום דגבי אשם כתיב והתודה לא סבר כסוגיא דהכא דאשם היינו קרבן ויליף במה הצד מפיו ועד אחד כדמסיק הכא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the apparent contradiction between this sugya, which implies that according to רבי מאיר witnesses obligate an אשם, and the Mishna in שבועות and בבא קמא which state that witnesses do not obligate an אשם, by suggesting that the Mishna there should be understood as referring to a case where the animal was טריפה, whereas this sugya maintains that witnesses do obligate an אשם according to רבי מאיר.

steps.title: Challenge from חֹמֵשׁ
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the kal vachomer by noting that a partial admission obligates a חומש (one-fifth penalty), whereas witnesses do not.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new objection against the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It points out a specific legal difference: a partial admission triggers the obligation to pay a חֹמֵשׁ, but testimony of witnesses does not.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The objection attacks the validity of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר by highlighting a 'מַה לְפִיו'. The admission is stronger in that it carries the financial penalty of חֹמֵשׁ. If the admission is stronger, the קַל וָחוֹמֶר might be invalid because the witnesses lack this specific strength.

keyTerms.meaning: One-fifth penalty added to restitution for certain sins.

steps.whatToRemember: The objection is based on the חֹמֵשׁ penalty associated with voluntary admission.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a standard 'מַה לְפִיו' objection structure in a קַל וָחוֹמֶר analysis.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the boundaries of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר by checking if the source case has unique strengths not shared by the target case.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא: מָה לְפִיו – שֶׁכֵּן מְחַיְּיבוֹ חוֹמֶשׁ.
phrases.english: Rather: What is notable about the admission of the mouth?
This is not difficult,

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי חִיָּיא כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר סְבִירָא לֵיהּ,
phrases.english: Rabbi Hiyya holds in accordance with Rabbi Meir,

phrases.aramaic: כִּי הֵיכִי דִּמְחַיֵּיב לֵיהּ קׇרְבָּן מִקַּל וָחוֹמֶר –
phrases.english: Just as he renders him liable to an offering via a fortiori inference —
he renders him liable to a fifth via a fortiori inference.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא: מָה לְפִיו – שֶׁכֵּן מְחַיְּיבוֹ חוֹמֶשׁ.

rashi.hebrew: חומש ואשם - אם נשבע וכפר ממון והודה כתיב או מכל אשר ישבע עליו לשקר וגו' (שם ה):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the obligation of חומש and אשם applies when one swore falsely regarding money and later admitted, as derived from the verse 'or concerning anything about which he swore falsely'.

steps.title: Challenge from הַכְחָשָׁה וְהַזָּמָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara raises a new objection: a partial admission is not subject to הכחשה or הזמה, whereas witnesses are.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a second objection to the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It points out that a partial admission is not subject to הַכְחָשָׁה or הַזָּמָה, but witnesses are subject to both.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection attacks the קַל וָחוֹמֶר from the opposite direction. Previously, the admission seemed stronger. Now, the admission seems weaker because it lacks the vulnerabilities of witnesses. If the source case is weaker in this regard, the קַל וָחוֹמֶר might fail.

keyTerms.meaning: Contradiction of testimony by other witnesses.

steps.whatToRemember: The objection focuses on the legal vulnerabilities of witnesses vs. the stability of an admission.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a 'מַה לְפִיו' objection showing the admission is weaker in a specific legal aspect.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the קַל וָחוֹמֶר by checking if the source case has unique weaknesses not shared by the target case.

phrases.english: Rather: What is notable about the admission of the mouth?
A neighbor is not subject to contradiction and refutation,
Would you say the same regarding witnesses who are subject to contradiction and refutation.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא: מָה לְפִיו – שֶׁכֵּן אֵינוֹ בְּהַכְחָשָׁה וּבַהֲזָמָה

rashi.hebrew: אינו בהכחשה ובהזמה - אם הודה לו במנה ובאו עדים והכחישוהו לומר אינך חייב לו כלום אינו נפטר בכך דהודאת בעל דין כמאה עדים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that a partial admission (פיו) is not subject to הכחשה because the principle הודאת בעל דין כמאה עדים means that even if witnesses contradict the admission, the defendant is not exempted.

rashi.hebrew: שישנן בהכחשה ובהזמה - שאם באו שנים עדים והכחישום או הזימום אין זה משלם ממון על פיהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that witnesses are subject to הכחשה and הזמה, meaning that if other witnesses contradict them or prove they conspired, the defendant does not have to pay based on their testimony.

steps.title: Resolution via עֵד אֶחָד
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the objection by deriving the law from the kal vachomer of a single witness, who also obligates an oath despite not obligating payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the objection by shifting the source of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. Instead of deriving from the admission directly, it derives from the case of a עֵד אֶחָד.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution changes the premise of the קַל וָחוֹמֶר. The previous objection attacked the admission-witnesses comparison. This answer says the derivation is actually from single witness to two witnesses. The single witness is a weaker case but still obligates an oath. Two witnesses are stronger, so they should obligate an oath.
steps.whatToRemember: The קַל וָחוֹמֶר is re-derived from the עֵד אֶחָד case.
steps.confusionAlert: The shift from admission to עֵד אֶחָד is a key pivot in the סוּגְיָא.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows the גמרא's flexibility in finding alternative derivations when one path is blocked.

phrases.english: Rather, it comes from one witness:

phrases.aramaic: וּמָה עֵד אֶחָד שֶׁאֵין מְחַיְּיבוֹ מָמוֹן,
phrases.english: And what of one witness who does not obligate him in money,
obligates him in an oath –
Witnesses who obligate him in money,
Is it not logical that they obligate him in an oath!

rashi.hebrew: מחייבו שבועה - טענו חבירו מנה לי בידך והוא אומר אין לך בידי כלום ועד אחד מעידו שהוא חייב לו הרי זה נשבע להכחיש את העד דכתיב (דברים יט) לא יקום עד אחד באיש וגו' לכל עון ולכל חטאת הוא דאינו קם אבל לשבועה קם (שבועות דף מ.):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the obligation of an oath by a single witness is derived from the verse in דברים יט, which states that a single witness cannot convict for עון or חטאת, but implies he can obligate an oath to contradict the witness, as established in שבועות דף מ.

steps.title: Challenge from scope of oath
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the single witness derivation: a single witness obligates an oath only on what he testifies to, whereas two witnesses obligate an oath on what is denied.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises an objection to the עֵד אֶחָד קַל וָחוֹמֶר. It points out that a single witness obligates an oath only regarding the specific matter he testifies about.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection attacks the עֵד אֶחָד derivation by highlighting a difference in the scope of the oath. The single witness's oath is limited to his testimony. The two witnesses' oath covers the denial, which may extend beyond their testimony. This difference undermines the קַל וָחוֹמֶר.
steps.whatToRemember: The objection focuses on the different scopes of the oaths in the two cases.
steps.confusionAlert: The distinction between 'on what he testifies' and 'on what he denies' is crucial.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the קַל וָחוֹמֶר by checking if the source and target cases share the same legal mechanism.

phrases.aramaic: מָה לְעֵד אֶחָד שֶׁכֵּן עַל מָה
phrases.english: What is one witness lacking, that a neighbor has?

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁהוּא מֵעִיד הוּא נִשְׁבָּע, תֹּאמַר בְּעֵדִים,
phrases.english: That he testifies, he is obligated to swear, would you say about witnesses,
That on whatever he denied, he is obligated to swear.

steps.triggerLanguage: מָה לְעֵד אֶחָד שֶׁכֵּן עַל מָה שֶׁהוּא מֵעִיד הוּא נִשְׁבָּע

rashi.hebrew: מה לעד אחד - כלומר היכי ילפת שבועה דשנים משבועה דעד אחד מה לעד אחד שכן על מה שהעד מעיד הוא נשבע להכחישו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the objection highlights a fundamental difference: a single witness's oath is limited to the specific matter he testifies about, whereas the oath derived for two witnesses would cover the denial, which may differ from their testimony.
